title: Star Anise and Cinnamon Braised Chicken

subs.name: 500 g firm tofu or soya chunks, for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: soak soya chunks in hot water for 15 minutes; reduce braising time to 12 minutes

subs.name: 1 tsp Chinese five-spice powder added with the soy sauce
subs.notes: five-spice powder is available at Chinese grocery stores; contains star anise, fennel, cinnamon, cloves and pepper

subs.name: salt is not a direct substitute; use soy sauce for authenticity
subs.notes: soy sauce provides both colour and umami — it is essential here

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: used to caramelise first, creating the sauce's rich dark colour
ingredients.notes: used to caramelise first, creating the sauce's rich dark colour

subs.name: 2 tbsp jaggery (vellam/gur) for caramelising
subs.notes: jaggery caramelises well and adds a deeper, more complex sweetness

ingredients.name: rice or white vinegar
visual_checks: a dash at the end brightens the braising sauce
ingredients.notes: a dash at the end brightens the braising sauce

steps.text: Pat the chicken pieces dry with a cloth. Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pot or kadai over medium-high heat. Brown the chicken in two batches, skin-side first, for 3 minutes per side until deep golden. Do not crowd the pot. Remove the browned chicken and set aside.

In the same pot over medium heat, add the sugar in a single layer. Watch it carefully — within 1–2 minutes it will melt and turn amber. This is the caramel that gives the dish its colour. If it starts to smell bitter, immediately add a splash of water to halt the process.
Add the garlic, ginger, star anise, cinnamon and bay leaves to the caramel. Stir for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Return the chicken to the pot. Add soy sauce and water. The liquid should come roughly halfway up the chicken. Bring to a boil, then cover and reduce to a low simmer.

Braise the chicken on low heat for 30 minutes, turning the pieces halfway through, until the meat is very tender and pulls slightly from the bone. The sauce should have reduced and thickened noticeably.

Pressure cooker shortcut: after adding liquid, close the lid and cook on high pressure for 3 whistles. Allow natural pressure release for 10 minutes, then open and reduce the sauce uncovered for 5 minutes on high heat.

Remove the star anise, cinnamon and bay leaves. Add a dash of vinegar if using. Taste and add salt if needed — soy sauce is salty, so be cautious. Serve the chicken with rice, spooning the glossy dark sauce generously over both.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Follow the cooker manufacturer's fill, venting and opening instructions; never force the lid open under pressure.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → 500 g firm tofu or soya chunks, for a vegetarian version (soak soya chunks in hot water for 15 minutes; reduce braising time to 12 minutes); star-anise → 1 tsp Chinese five-spice powder added with the soy sauce (five-spice powder is available at Chinese grocery stores; contains star anise, fennel, cinnamon, cloves and pepper); sugar → 2 tbsp jaggery (vellam/gur) for caramelising (jaggery caramelises well and adds a deeper, more complex sweetness); soy-sauce → salt is not a direct substitute; use soy sauce for authenticity (soy sauce provides both colour and umami — it is essential here)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Star anise (annachi mokku) and cinnamon (pattai) are everyday spices in Indian kitchens, making this dish remarkably easy to source. Soy sauce is the only ingredient that may need a special trip, but it is available at most Indian supermarkets. The pressure cooker method (3 whistles + natural release) cuts cooking time dramatically. A kadai works well for the initial browning; use a heavy pot for the braise or transfer to a pressure cooker. Serve with rice for a deeply satisfying meal.
